active_task.goal: Explain how "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" (second paper) builds on "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" (first paper), grounded in the selected evidence. The explanation should cover how the second extends/specializes the first in scope, taxonomy, perspective, benchmarks, frameworks, methodology, and trends, and is grounded in the comparison_matrix obligations already established for the active deep_comparison task.

run_live_chat_59fa12a64872:726cfbbd5683c.text: benchmarks: reviews existing datasets and methodologies for LLM-based multi-turn conversational agents; 276 papers offering targeted evaluation insights

end-to-end vs component perspective: end-to-end first — prioritizes holistic end-to-end experience at the dialogue level, then drills into granular metrics (task completion, response quality, user experience, memory/context retention, planning/tool integration)

frameworks: organizes evaluation methodologies into annotation-based, automated metrics, hybrid human+quantitative, and self-judging LLM methods, including traditional metrics like BLEU and ROUGE alongside interactive multi-turn techniques

methodology: PRISMA-inspired systematic review from 2017 to 2025; initial Google Scholar search of 479 papers plus 1,081 venue papers, yielding 351 papers for qualitative synthesis and 276 with targeted evaluation insights

taxonomy: two interrelated taxonomies — "what to evaluate" (task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, planning and tool integration) and "how to evaluate" (annotation-based, automated metrics, hybrid human+quantitative, self-judging LLM methods)

run_live_chat_59fa12a64872:8b9e2f64d5651.text: benchmarks: application-specific benchmarks for web, software engineering, scientific, and conversational agents, plus generalist agent benchmarks
frameworks: evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers
methodology: rigorous multi-stage literature review with three phases — systematic search, structured selection, and expert validation
scope: evaluation of LLM-based agents broadly

run_live_chat_91b660e4455c:corpus_covera.text: The corpus of two evaluation-focused surveys is sufficient to ground a narrower recommendation set on the evaluation of LLM-based agents and multi-turn conversational agents, but it is not sufficient to ground a general "important papers on LLM-based agents" recommendation covering the broader literature (planning, tool use, memory, multi-agent systems, general agent architectures) outside the evaluation sub-area.

run_live_chat_91b660e4455c:corpus_in_sto.text: Only the two evaluation surveys themselves are present in the user-visible corpus: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" and "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey." The other works cited or discussed in these surveys (e.g., AgentBench, HAL, MTU-Bench, MMDU, API-Bank, BotChat, HUMOD, Gaia, MetaTool) appear only as references within those surveys and are not in the inventory as standalone items.

run_live_chat_91b660e4455c:scope_def_1.text: Recommendations are scoped to the evaluation of LLM-based agents, covering sub-areas such as core capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory), application-specific agent benchmarks (web, SWE, scientific, conversational), generalist agent benchmarks, and multi-turn conversation evaluation.

run_live_chat_c1cece2ffc88:paper1_benchm.text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" surveys benchmarks across multiple categories: fundamental capability benchmarks (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory); application-specific benchmarks for web agents, software engineering agents, scientific agents, and conversational agents; and generalist agent benchmarks such as Gaia, Gaia2, AgentBench, and the HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard). The survey observes a shift from application-specific toward general-purpose agents that integrate planning, reasoning, tool use, web interaction, file handling, and code execution.

run_live_chat_c1cece2ffc88:paper1_e2e_vs.text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" covers both end-to-end and component-level evaluation. It organizes the field by agent capabilities (planning, tool use, self-reflection, memory) and by application domains, and explicitly identifies as a future direction the need for "advancing granular evaluation" beyond coarse-grained end-to-end success metrics, calling for standardized, fine-grained, step-by-step assessment of agent trajectories such as tool selection and reasoning quality.

run_live_chat_c1cece2ffc88:paper1_scope.text: The paper "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" defines its scope as a comprehensive survey of evaluation methods for LLM-based agents, covering five perspectives: (1) core LLM capabilities for agentic workflows, (2) application-specific benchmarks such as web and SWE agents, (3) evaluation of generalist agents, (4) analysis of core benchmark dimensions, and (5) evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers. It frames the field as a paradigm shift from static text-to-text LLMs to autonomous agents that plan, reason, and use tools in dynamic environments.

run_live_chat_c1cece2ffc88:paper1_taxono.text: "A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents" analyzes agent benchmarks along shared orthogonal core dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety. Within safety and robustness, it highlights metrics such as pass^k (fraction of tasks successful across all k independent runs) and the need for "guardrail" metrics that penalize agents achieving task success via non-compliant actions like policy violations. The taxonomy is structural and cross-cutting rather than organized around what/how to evaluate.

run_live_chat_c1cece2ffc88:paper2_benchm.text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" surveys holistic multi-turn dialogue benchmarks organized into categories such as dialogue modeling (HUMOD, BotChat, AgentBench), and tool and action integration (API-Bank, MMDU, MT-Mind2Web, MTU-Bench, Factcheck-Bench). The survey emphasizes benchmarks that assess realism, domain knowledge, long-horizon reasoning, multi-turn tool usage, user intent understanding, and factual veracity in conversational contexts.

run_live_chat_c1cece2ffc88:paper2_e2e_vs.text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" argues that multi-turn dialogues necessitate evaluating at the dialogue level rather than individual exchanges, prioritizing end-to-end experience because this holistic approach captures real-world performance. Complementing this, it devotes explicit attention to component-level evaluation of action and tool-use components, including multi-step tool selection and reasoning benchmarks such as MetaTool/ToolE, MTU-Bench, and program-synthesis and mathematical reasoning benchmarks tailored to dialogue contexts.

run_live_chat_c1cece2ffc88:paper2_scope.text: The paper "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" defines its scope as the evaluation of LLM-based agents specifically in multi-turn conversational settings, covering task-oriented and open-domain dialogue systems. It situates these systems historically from ELIZA through statistical and Transformer-based models to modern LLM-powered agents, and emphasizes the unique challenges of automated, reproducible, human-aligned evaluation for context-aware multi-turn interactions such as customer service and personal assistants.

run_live_chat_c1cece2ffc88:paper2_taxono.text: "Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey" develops two interrelated taxonomy systems. The "what to evaluate" taxonomy identifies key components: task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, and planning and tool integration. The "how to evaluate" taxonomy categorizes methodologies into annotation-based evaluations, automated metrics (including BLEU, ROUGE, and BERTScore), hybrid strategies combining human and quantitative assessments, and self-judging methods utilizing LLMs.

ev_007b64413ba635bc.span_text: To better reflect real-world scenarios, recent evaluations have become increasingly agentic (Patil et al., 2025a), requiring longer interactions and scaling the number of domains and tools by sourcing them from real MCP servers (Anthropic, Nov 2024). Two current frontier benchmarks,
ev_007b64413ba635bc.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_0fcd65f08e2c1e3f.span_text: The second approach for generalist agent evaluation relies on unifying several task-specific benchmarks into one. AgentBench (Liu et al., 2023) introduces interactive environments spanning OS operations, databases, games, and household tasks�highlighting core skills like flexibility and tool-based problem solving. HAL (Holistic Agent Leaderboard; Stroebl et al., 2025) provides a unified platform for benchmarks across domains, including coding and web. Yet, this approach does not support the same agent harness evaluation across any benchmark environment. Harbor and Exgentic aim to solve that by providing frameworks with a unified protocol for general agent assessment (Harbor Framework Team, 2026; Bandel et al., 2026a). These represent the first steps toward holistic, standardized, cross-environment agent evaluation (Lacoste et al., 2026).
ev_0fcd65f08e2c1e3f.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_1c64675d2937d706.span_text: Historically, multi-turn dialogue dates back to ELIZA [232], developed in the 1960s, which maintained simple states through pattern matching rather than complex context modeling. Subsequent progress incorporated finite-state machines, frame-based approaches, and statistical methods, culminating in the application of hidden Markov models (HMMs) [178] for spoken language understanding. Deep neural networks and Transformer architectures [217] then revolutionized context modeling, leveraging larger datasets and advanced training methods (including supervised and reinforcement learning) to handle more complex dialogues. In nowadays, multi-turn conversational systems often build on large-scale pre
ev_1c64675d2937d706.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_1f2ecd2f2c65b6ec.span_text: Multi-turn conversational agents powered by large language models (LLMs) mark a significant leap in conversational AI [74], ofering complex, context-aware interactions across multiple turns�essential for applications like customer service and personal assistants [38, 235]. Evaluating these systems poses unique challenges: methods must be automated, reproducible, and aligned with human judgments to ensure real-world reliability [247].
ev_1f2ecd2f2c65b6ec.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_23dcc76307d339ca.span_text: One approach aims to address this gap by proposing benchmarks that inherently require a wide range of capabilities. A key benchmark in this category is Gaia (Mialon et al., 2023). Gaia is composed of real-world questions that require abilities such as reasoning, multi-modality handling, web browsing, and tool-use proficiency. Over time, model performance on Gaia improved, saturating the easier portion of the benchmark and emphasizing the need for an updated version. Andrews et al. (2025) proposed Gaia2 as a mobile environment with apps such as email, messaging, and calendar. The set of required capabilities was extended to include handling ambiguity, noise, temporal constraints, and multi-ag
ev_23dcc76307d339ca.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_28a5184ea0d65277.span_text: Our taxonomy fills these gaps by systematically exploring what aspects to evaluate and how. We define comprehensive goals, discuss data-driven annotation and automatic evaluation, and study robust metrics in multi-turn evaluation. Unlike earlier work [11, 35, 156, 247], our framework addresses future challenges (e.g., scalability, real-world applicability) and ofers a structured, holistic approach to assessing LLM-based multi-turn conversational agents. This perspective is vital for building reliable, adaptable dialogue systems capable of handling complex multi-turn interactions efectively.
ev_28a5184ea0d65277.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_2e63d32c8d5ba8c8.span_text: SWE-bench (Jimenez et al., 2023) was introduced to address the above shortcomings by providing an end-to-end evaluation framework grounded in real-world GitHub issues. Each task includes a detailed issue description, full repository context, executable environments (e.g., Docker), and validation tests, enabling agents to generate and verify code patches automatically. Several follow-up works identified evaluation issues, like overly specific or unrelated unit tests, underspecified issue descriptions, and problematic environmental setups, and thus proposed new variants (SWE-bench Lite, 2024; Xia et al., 2024; OpenAI, 2024; Aleithan et al., 2024). The most widely adopted of these, SWE-bench Verified (OpenAI, 2024), carries extensive human filtration and validation to yield a high-quality subset of 500 samples. It also standardizes execution through containerized environments and provides difficulty annotations, enabling more reproducible and interpretable evaluations. These improvements make SWE-bench Verified the de facto benchmark for assessing SWE agents.
ev_2e63d32c8d5ba8c8.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_2f58e77634814fd0.section: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey
ev_2f58e77634814fd0.span_text: Additional Key Words and Phrases: large language models, agent, multi-turn conversation system, task-oriented dialogue systems, open-domain dialogue systems, evaluation
ev_2f58e77634814fd0.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_3362366e1317e9a9.span_text: Realistic and Challenging Evaluation. While early evaluations often employed simplified, static environments, there is a clear shift toward more realistic and complex benchmarks. Web agents evolved from basic simulations to dynamic, realworld settings such as WebArena. In software engineering, benchmarks like SWE-bench utilize real GitHub issues, moving beyond synthetic coding tasks. Furthermore, there is increasing interest in long-horizon tasks typically performed by highly trained human experts, pushing evaluation closer to real-world professional workflows. This change reflects the growing need to assess agents� ability to derive practical value in realistic settings, to stresstest their limitations, and guide their progress.
ev_3362366e1317e9a9.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_394a88565bab8e83.span_text: To ensure the comprehensiveness and validity of this survey, we employed a rigorous, multi-stage literature review process designed to capture the rapidly evolving landscape of LLM agent evaluation. Our methodology consisted of three primary phases: systematic search, structured selection, and expert validation.
ev_394a88565bab8e83.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_3a7de85f0d242c83.span_text: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
ev_3a7de85f0d242c83.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_3e731d621272672c.section: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey
ev_3e731d621272672c.span_text: This survey examines evaluation methods for large language model (LLM)-based agents in multi-turn conversational settings. Using a PRISMA-inspired framework, we systematically reviewed nearly 250 scholarly sources, capturing the state of the art from various venues of publication, and establishing a solid foundation for our analysis. Our study ofers a structured approach by developing two interrelated taxonomy systems: one that defines what to evaluate and another that explains how to evaluate. The first taxonomy identifies key components of LLM-based agents for multi-turn conversations and their evaluation dimensions, including task completion, response quality, user experience, memory and context retention, as well as planning and tool integration. These components ensure that the performance of conversational agents is assessed in a holistic and meaningful manner. The second taxonomy system focuses on the evaluation methodologies. It categorizes approaches into annotation-based evaluations, automated metrics, hybrid strategies that combine human assessments with quantitative measures, and self-judging methods utilizing LLMs. This framework not only captures traditional metrics derived from language understanding, such as BLEU and ROUGE scores, but also incorporates advanced techniques that reflect the dynamic, interactive nature of multi-turn dialogues. Together, these frameworks summarize the current status quo, expose limitations in traditional practices, and provide a structured blueprint for improvement. Based on the summarization of existing studies, we identify several challenges and propose future directions, including the development of scalable, real-time evaluation pipelines, enhanced privacy-preserving mechanisms, and robust metrics that capture dynamic multi-turn interactions. Our contributions bridge historical insights with modern practices, paving the way for next-generation, reliably evaluated conversational AI systems and ofering a comprehensive guide for researchers and practitioners
ev_3e731d621272672c.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_4626c986e6937cad.span_text: Advancing Granular Evaluation. Many current benchmarks rely on coarse-grained, end-to-end success metrics that, while useful for gauging overall performance, fall short in diagnosing specific agent failures. This lack of granularity obscures insights into intermediate decision processes such as tool selection and reasoning quality. Addressing this limitation calls for the development of standardized, fine-grained evaluation metrics targeting the trajectory of an agent�s task execution. Future work should explore detailed, step-by-step assessments to provide richer feedback and guide targeted improvements.
ev_4626c986e6937cad.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_5c1285d80b129c84.span_text: This paper provides a comprehensive overview of evaluation methods for LLM-based agents in multi-turn conversations. By systematically examining the evaluation goals�such as dialogue coherence, efective tool use, and memory retention�and the diverse methodologies, including annotation-based assessments and automated metrics, the study ofers a detailed synthesis of the progression from rule-based systems to transformer-based agents. This work lays a solid foundation for future explorations in the domain of conversational AI by addressing both theoretical and practical aspects. The core contribution of this paper lies in developing a structured taxonomy that clarifies critical dimensions of multi-turn conversational performance. This framework not only defines what elements should be evaluated but also establishes the methodological steps necessary to assess these components reliably. By synthesizing insights from nearly 200 scholarly sources, the study bridges the gap between existing evaluation methods and the emerging complexities inherent in modern LLM-based agents.
ev_5c1285d80b129c84.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_675fca9661b26bfa.span_text: This paper surveys evaluation methods for LLM-based multi-turn conversational agents, focusing on (1) which aspects to evaluate and (2) how to evaluate them. It outlines key targets (task success, response quality, end-to-end user experience) and reviews existing methodologies and datasets, encompassing both automatic metrics and human evaluation protocols. Drawing on literature from 2017 to 2025, this survey follows a methodology inspired by PRISMA [165], outlined in Figure 2. An initial Google Scholar search retrieved 479 papers<sup>1</sup>, supplemented by 1,081 more from venues such as ICLR, NeurIPS, AAAI, NAACL, EMNLP, and ACL (2022�2024). After merging duplicates (1,123 unique records), we excluded 333 irrelevant works, leaving 790 papers; of these, 267 lacked focused coverage of LLM-based agents and were excluded. Ultimately, 351 papers progressed to qualitative synthesis, with 276 ofering targeted evaluation insights.
ev_675fca9661b26bfa.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_7e8e750b159d2872.span_text: Scientific agents automate core research tasks by integrating domain knowledge and scientific tools. Their evaluation has progressed from basic reasoning benchmarks to comprehensive frameworks assessing diverse scientific research capabilities. Early efforts focused on knowledge recall and reasoning (Clark et al., 2018; Lu et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022a), and literature understanding (Dasigi et al., 2021; Lee et al., 2023; DeYoung et al., 2021). More recent benchmarks like SciRiff (Wadden et al., 2024) broaden the scope to instructionfollowing across scientific domains.
ev_7e8e750b159d2872.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_8eb726c4c50ad455.span_text: Inclusion and Exclusion Criteria We defined strict criteria to maintain the survey�s focus and quality. Papers were included if they introduced a novel benchmark, an evaluation framework, or a significant methodological contribution to the assessment of LLM-based agents. Conversely, we excluded works that: 1. Proposed new agent architectures without a distinct contribution to evaluation methodology. 2. Focused solely on traditional LLM evaluation (e.g., reasoning on static datasets), lacking dynamic, agentic, or interactive components. While acceptance to top-tier conferences was utilized as a primary indicator of quality, we also included high-impact preprints to ensure the survey reflects the most recent advancements in this fast-paced field.
ev_8eb726c4c50ad455.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_94d09c1772db0a83.span_text: The shift from static models to adaptive, interactive agents calls for a new paradigm for evaluating LLM-based agents. Such evaluation must go beyond measuring LLM textual outputs to assess an agent�s capacity for sequential decision-making and operation within dynamic environments. It requires benchmarks that can assess the agent�s ability to accomplish user tasks via a sequence of actions and interactions. Moreover, benchmarks must co-evolve with agent capabilities, accommodating new classes of tasks and domains.
ev_94d09c1772db0a83.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_97e56b2d94b70372.span_text: Robust evaluation metrics serve as the critical yardstick for quantifying agent intelligence and interaction quality, necessitating a framework that encompasses both rigorous annotation-based benchmarks and scalable annotation-free methodologies. This section reviews evaluation metrics and methods for assessing multi-turn conversational agents, as briefly illustrated in Figure 8 and Figure 9 provides a concrete example of how these metrics are applied in practice. The evaluation approaches are primarily divided into two categories: Annotation-based Evaluation, which relies on pre-labeled data to benchmark responses, and Annotation-free Evaluation, which automates scoring without the need for manual labels. Annotation-based methods provide detailed, human-curated insights into dialogue quality but are resource-intensive and can be limited by subjective biases in annotation. In contrast, annotation-free metrics leverage self-supervised learning and real-time adaptation to ofer scalable evaluations across diverse dialogue contexts, although they may initially require robust training data for optimal performance. This shift towards automation in evaluation not only reduces costs but also enhances scalability, making it feasible to assess conversational agents in real-world, dynamic environments with greater eficiency and adaptability. A comparison between two method is at Table 1.
ev_97e56b2d94b70372.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_985210f77a60dcf4.section: 2.1 Evaluating the End-to-end Experience of LLM-based Agents For Multi-Turn Conversation
ev_985210f77a60dcf4.span_text: It is natural to prioritize evaluating the end-to-end experience in the first place, as this holistic approach directly captures real-world performance before delving into granular metrics. Multi-turn dialogues inherently difer from isolated single-turn queries in that they often encompass multiple sequential or interleaved tasks within a single conversation. This complexity necessitates evaluating an agent�s performance at the dialogue level, beyond individua exchanges. Specifically, we must assess not only whether each task is completed but also how well tasks are executed. In other words, even if an agent successfully fulfills each requested task, we still examine the overall quality of task completion and the interaction process: does the agent transition smoothly between tasks, maintain context and coherence, and deliver responses that keep the user satisfied and engaged without producing harmful or irrelevan content? For example, an agent may correctly answer each question (achieving high task completion) but still fail overall if its responses are confusing, inconsistent, or inappropriate. Hence, We present an overview of End-to-end Experience evaluation goals for LLM-based agents in multi-turn interactions. The representative works could be categorized into five dimensions: (1) Task Completion in Multi-Turn Conversations, measuring how well agents fulfill user requests using metrics like completion rate; (2) Multitask Capabilities, assessing expertise across domains such as mathematics, coding and etc; (3) Interaction Patterns, analyzing structures like recollection, follow-up, and expansion to assess dialogue coherence and (4) User Experience and Safety, addressing satisfaction, engagement, and safeguards against harmful content, including adversarial attacks and prompt leakage.
ev_985210f77a60dcf4.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_9ed6b1fff3669080.span_text: Existing surveys in the related areas have distinct focuses but often leave critical gaps. For example, Arora et al. [11] give only a brief overview of dialogue systems, while classifications into task-oriented vs. non-task-oriented models can overlook agent�system interactions [35]. Recent surveys [8, 80, 156, 222, 247] each tackle diferent perspectives bu do not address interactions between agentic components in detail or the unique challenges of multi-turn evaluations. Li [115] discuss LLM-based agent capabilities without focusing on multi-turn dialogue, and Chang et al. [31] review broader LLM evaluation dimensions without delving into multi-turn specifics. Zhang et al. [250] discuss evaluators, such as GPT-4 for assessing multi-turn interactions. However, it lacks detailed descriptions of both annotation-based and annotation-free evaluation methods.
ev_9ed6b1fff3669080.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_aead9018f3eb7236.section: 2.2 Evaluating The Action and Tool-use Components of LLM-based Agents for Multi-turn Conversation
ev_aead9018f3eb7236.span_text: 2.2.2 Multi-step Tool Selection and Reasoning . Multi-step tool use requires the agent�s "chain of thought" in action. I is not enough to select the right tool once; the agent must demonstrate contextual awareness to know when tools are needed, sequential logic to order them correctly across turns, and domain resilience to maintain this logic in specialized fields like math or coding. MetaTool [85] evaluates LLMs� tool awareness and decision-making using the ToolE dataset, which tests single- and multi-tool scenarios. It highlights challenges in tool selection, revealing gaps in LLM capabilities Wang et al. [225] introduced MTU-Bench to assess multi-tool usage and multi-step interactions across scenarios, including single- and multi-turn tasks, enhancing action execution in conversations. Nijkamp et al. [157] proposed a program synthesis benchmark focusing on breaking down complex programming tasks, while Wang et al. [226] developed a Python-based benchmark for evaluating tool-use in responses. Mathematical reasoning benchmarks were introduced by Kurtic et al. [102] and Sun et al. [207], focusing on dialogue contexts. Guo et al. [68] designed a benchmark for assessing multi-turn tool-use in Chinese societal applications, and Zhuang et al. [275] emphasized open-ended tool usage in question answering. Lastly, Huang et al. [81] proposed a comprehensive benchmark to evaluate the full tool-use process, including planning and creation in complex tasks. These studies suggest that a unified evaluation framework for multi-step tool selection and reasoning should assess invocation precision, operational eficiency, and error recovery. It should also evaluate tool awareness, planning capability, execution accuracy, and multi-turn coherence.
ev_aead9018f3eb7236.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_be9ffa7f121ef2a8.span_text: Safety and Robustness Metrics While most benchmarks prioritize capability, safety, and robustness are critical for enterprise adoption. Robustness is often quantified via pass^k, the fraction of tasks where the agent succeeds across all k independent runs. Beyond robustness, enterprise agents must adhere to strict policies, such as data privacy and access control, which are rarely tested in standard benchmarks. For instance, SWE-Lancer does not inherently penalize risky behaviors unless they interfere with replicating the target behavior. Future benchmarks must integrate "guardrail" metrics, penalizing agents that achieve task success via non-compliant actions (e.g., deleting production data
ev_be9ffa7f121ef2a8.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_c750d2148fe35440.span_text: Search Strategy and Citation Chasing Our initial data collection involved a systematic search across major academic repositories and archives, including Google Scholar, the ACL Anthology, HuggingFace Papers, and arXiv. We utilized a targeted set of keywords and their combinations, such as �LLM agent evaluation,� �agent benchmark,� �tool use evaluation,� and �web agent benchmark.� Following the identification of seminal and highly cited works, we applied an iterative �snowballing� technique. We performed both forward and backward citation chasing on these seed papers to uncover foundational prior art as well as emerging methodologies that might not yet be indexed by standard keywords.
ev_c750d2148fe35440.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_c8451279c5aff7c2.span_text: Similar to the evolution of LLMs from task-specific into general-purpose models, agents are also transitioning from application-specific toward generalpurpose agents (Bandel et al., 2026b). Such tasks require integrated capabilities, from planning, reasoning, and tool use to web interaction, file handling, code execution, and more. This shift has led to the development of two complementary approaches to generalist agent evaluation.
ev_c8451279c5aff7c2.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_ceb02e5ea599218f.span_text: We begin by discussing the evaluation of fundamental LLM-based agent capabilities (�2). These include planning, tool use, self-reflection, and memory. We then review benchmarks and evaluation strategies for prominent types of agentic applications: web agents, software engineering agents, scientific agents, and conversational agents (�3). We continue to describe benchmarks and leaderboards for evaluating generalist agents (�4). Consequently, we define and analyze core dimensions of agent benchmarks (�5). �6 reviews current evaluation frameworks for agent developers. These frameworks integrate with the agent�s development environment, and support its evaluation throughout the entire development cycle. We conclude with a discussion (�7) of current trends and future research directions in agent evaluation. Figure 1 offers a visual summary of the survey�s structure.
ev_ceb02e5ea599218f.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_cef6745d7b152804.span_text: The preceding sections reviewed benchmarks organized by agent type and application domain. Here, we take a broader perspective and analyze benchmarks along shared orthogonal dimensions: data curation, environment, interface, metric, and safety. This analysis reveals common structural patterns and gaps. In Table 1, we compare representative benchmarks based on these dimensions.
ev_cef6745d7b152804.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_cf8f947b23acb5ce.span_text: � Holistic Evaluation Benchmarks and Datasets: As LLMs advanced, the field shifted toward more comprehensive benchmarks that incorporate human judgments, advanced automatic metrics such as BERTScore [97, 258], and task-specific evaluations to reflect the increasingly complex, context-rich nature of multi-turn interactions [10, 46]. The complexity of multi-turn dialogues led to the creation of specialized datasets and benchmarks tha focus on realism, domain knowledge, and long-horizon reasoning: (1) Dialogue Modeling: HUMOD [145] for human-like dialogue modeling; BotChat [50] for open-ended chat scenarios; AgentBench [133] for agent-centric tasks. (2) Tool and Action Integration: API-Bank [114] and MMDU [135] assess real-world tool usage, while MT-Mind2Web [46], MTU-Bench [225], and Factcheck-Bench [228] provide structured evaluations of multi-turn reasoning, user intent, and factual veracity.
ev_cf8f947b23acb5ce.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_ddb084a0ece5ad74.span_text: LLM-based agents represent a paradigm shift in AI, enabling autonomous systems to plan, reason, and use tools while interacting with dynamic environments. This paper provides the first comprehensive survey of evaluation methods for these increasingly capable agents. We analyze the field of agent evaluation across five perspectives: (1) Core LLM capabilities needed for agentic workflows, like planning, and tool use; (2) Application-specific benchmarks such as web and SWE agents; (3) Evaluation of generalist agents; (4) Analysis of agent benchmarks� core dimensions; and (5) Evaluation frameworks and tools for agent developers. Our analysis reveals current trends, including a shift toward more realistic, challenging evaluations with continuously updated benchmarks. We also identify critical gaps that future research must address�particularly in assessing cost-efficiency, safety, and robustness, and in developing fine-grained, scalable evaluation methods <sup>1</sup>.
ev_ddb084a0ece5ad74.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_e0b3c0e1ded59f14.span_text: This survey presents an overview of the evolving field of LLM-based agent evaluation, outlining its progression from assessing isolated capabilities in simplified settings to evaluating agents in realistic, dynamic, and challenging environments. While notable progress has been made, several challenges remain. Future research should focus on developing fine-grained, scalable evaluation methods that extend beyond overall success rates, establishing standardized metrics for cost-efficiency, safety, and robustness for responsible deployment. For practical guidance, we provide actionable benchmark recommendations in Appendix �E.
ev_e0b3c0e1ded59f14.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_e5e53ffde8fe3ba4.section: 3 How to evaluate? The evaluation methodologies and data
ev_e5e53ffde8fe3ba4.span_text: Evaluating LLM-based agent in multi-turn scenario involves a variety of methodologies, each with its own strengths and limitations. These systems are complex, requiring evaluation methods that can capture their dynamic and interactive nature. Table 2 and Table 3 summarize recent related benchmarks and datasets.
ev_e5e53ffde8fe3ba4.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_eb00cd688a869556.span_text: LLMs have recently made remarkable progress, tackling a wide range of challenging tasks. Yet, LLMs are static, having fixed knowledge, and confined to text-to-text interaction. LLM-based agents address those gaps by building on LLMs as a backbone, integrating them into multi-step workflows and equipping them with external tools (Wang et al., 2024a). Hence, LLM agents can perform computations, retrieve up-to-date information, and interact with their environment. Crucially, they can autonomously plan, execute, and adapt complex strategies in real-world settings. This agency enables them to tackle problems once beyond the reach of AI, unlocking innovative applications across diverse domains.
ev_eb00cd688a869556.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents

ev_f89edd5f4bf66e37.span_text: Figure 1 illustrates an agent-based conversational system. Here, the user plans a trip to Europe; the agent suggests destinations, acknowledges a selection (Paris), confirms dates, and provides accommodation options. The agent�s planning process [84] breaks the request into actionable steps, narrowing the destination, confirming dates, and searching for relevant hotels. Tool-use [57, 161, 174], such as invoking search\_web for internet queries, yields structured data integrated into the final response. Two types of memory [24, 130]�conversation and turn memory�preserve dialogue context [259, 261]. The LLM interprets queries, formats responses, and manages complex language tasks [155], ensuring coherently adapted answers based on user inputs.
ev_f89edd5f4bf66e37.title: Evaluating LLM-based Agents for Multi-Turn Conversations: A Survey

ev_fc60e9cc9fdd5a6c.span_text: Expert Consultation To mitigate coverage gaps and ensure an accurate representation of specific subdomains, we consulted with domain experts corresponding to the key categories in our taxonomy (e.g., SWE and web agent researchers). We specifically engaged with creators of leading benchmarks and dominant agent architectures to discuss the selected manuscripts. This validation step ensured that our survey captures the nuances of state-of-theart evaluation protocols.
ev_fc60e9cc9fdd5a6c.title: A Survey on Evaluation of LLM-based Agents
